commit 2ef3f76cfd79821109155d06be961dff54a78b60
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Jun 5 01:06:10 2026 +0530

    perf: cache parsing and betweenness across incremental updates (#369)
    
    * perf(cli): keep the generation stack out of index-only updates
    
    Index-only updates (the post-commit hook hot path) imported
    ContextAssembler/PageGenerator before the index-only branch, and even
    leaf imports like generation.report paid for the full package __init__.
    Move the generation import + GenerationConfig construction below the
    index-only return, and make the package exports lazy (PEP 562) so light
    leaves no longer pull the assembler/generator/editor_files stack.
    Guarded by a subprocess test asserting generation.report leaves the
    heavy modules unloaded.
    
    * fix(cli): honor the persisted git tier on incremental updates
    
    update_cmd constructed GitIndexer without a tier, so every update ran
    FULL per-file blame — even for fast-mode (ESSENTIAL) repos whose index
    deliberately skipped it. Thread state.json's git_tier through
    _rebuild_graph_and_git; unknown/missing values keep the historical FULL
    behavior for legacy state files.
    
    * perf(ingestion): content-hash parse cache for unchanged files
    
    Parsing is the repo-proportional term of every ingest: repowise update
    re-parses the whole repo when one file changed, and large-repo inits pay
    minutes of tree-sitter time for files that haven't moved. Cache
    ParsedFiles keyed by (path, content hash) under a parser fingerprint
    (.scm query sources + package version), stored as pickle snapshots taken
    at parse time — graph builds mutate ParsedFile in place, so hits
    deserialize a fresh object graph and rebind the current FileInfo.
    
    Wired into the init pipeline (_run_ingestion + reparse_for_resume; hits
    skip the worker pool entirely) and the update path's _build_repo_graph.
    Best-effort throughout: corrupt cache, stale fingerprint, or any cache
    error degrades silently to a full parse; the cache file is rewritten
    with only entries touched per run so deleted files age out.
    
    Update-path ingest on this repo: 6.8s cold -> 2.7s warm (isolated).
    Equivalence suite: cached run is ParsedFile-equal + graph-isomorphic to
    a from-scratch build after edit/add/delete/rename/import-changing edit.
    
    * perf(graph): reuse betweenness when the subgraph structure is unchanged
    
    Exact Brandes betweenness was the largest remaining block of an
    incremental update (~8s on this repo, recomputed every run even for
    docstring-only edits). Betweenness is a pure function of the unweighted
    subgraph structure, so cache the values on disk keyed by a signature
    over the sorted node + edge sets: content edits that don't move
    call/heritage/import edges reuse the previous run's exact values; any
    structural change misses and recomputes as before.
    
    Opt-in via GraphBuilder(centrality_cache_dir=...) — wired from the init
    pipeline and the update path; ad-hoc builders are unchanged. Thread-safe
    (file + symbol kinds compute concurrently) and best-effort: corrupt or
    missing cache falls back to a fresh computation. For >30k-node graphs
    the sampled-betweenness path also caches, which makes its previously
    seedless (run-to-run varying) values stable for an unchanged graph.
    
    Update wall on this repo: both kinds reused, ~31s -> ~19s with warm
    parse + centrality caches.

commit d0f2315c87ea43683e719af73e48b3ae31b96345
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Jun 5 00:08:38 2026 +0530

    perf: make indexing & incremental update scale with change size (#368)
    
    * perf(pipeline): read source bytes with a thread pool before parsing
    
    The parse phase read every file sequentially before handing bytes to the
    ProcessPoolExecutor. Extract the loop into _read_sources() backed by a
    ThreadPoolExecutor (I/O-bound), preserving input order, per-file error
    handling, and parse-bar ticks. Applies to both _run_ingestion and
    reparse_for_resume.
    
    * perf(graph): cache file/symbol subgraphs across metric kernels
    
    file_subgraph()/symbol_subgraph() were rebuilt (full filtered copy,
    O(V+E)) on every metric call - seven kernels between them. Cache the
    filtered copies per build behind a lock (init computes metrics
    concurrently via asyncio.to_thread), invalidate on every structural
    mutation (build/add_file, co-change refresh, dynamic + framework edges)
    and on release_graph so the memory release stays effective.
    
    Also fold compute_percentiles' is_hotspot + entropy-default passes into
    the rank-assignment loop (one scan instead of three; same output).
    
    * perf(ingestion): one pruned walk for ts_workspace file discovery
    
    The mdx / vitest-config / package.json finders each rglob'd the entire
    tree - 12+ full walks with no pruning of node_modules, virtualenvs or
    .git. Measured at ~365s of a 737s --index-only init on a medium repo
    with a checked-in .venv; any repo with node_modules installed pays the
    same on every init and update.
    
    Replace them with a single os.walk (memoized per ResolverContext) that
    prunes vendored/derived dirs and collects all three target sets in one
    pass: 365s -> 0.02s on the same tree. The npm-script finder now derives
    package-relative paths against the unresolved repo_path to match the
    shared scan's base.
    
    * perf(update): pre-compute graph metrics with fan-out parallelism
    
    The update path's persist_graph_nodes computed pagerank/betweenness/
    symbol variants/communities lazily and sequentially. Reuse the init
    path's compute_metrics_parallel() at the end of _rebuild_graph_and_git
    (after the co-change edge refresh so cached subgraphs see the final
    structure) and in the config-triggered full health rescore. Best-effort:
    all metrics still fall back to lazy computation.
    
    * perf(graph): O(1) Tier-2b resolution + shared import-name index
    
    Tier-2b in both resolvers scanned every imported file's symbol dict per
    unresolved call/heritage ref, and the trait-dispatch fallback scanned
    every file in the repo per unresolved member call. Replace with:
    
    - lazy per-file merged {name -> symbol_id} / {(class,method) -> id} /
      {type -> id} views of the imported files' tables, merged in sorted
      path order with first-wins. The old set iteration order was
      hash-randomized per process, so shadowed names resolved
      non-deterministically; sorted order makes the winner stable.
    - a global {(class,method) -> [(path, id)]} index in file-insertion
      order for trait dispatch, preserving the old first-match identity
      and the skip-own-file rule.
    
    The byte-identical import-name maps both resolvers built independently
    now come from import_index.build_import_name_maps, constructed once per
    GraphBuilder.build() and injected (constructors keep a build-it-locally
    fallback, and the builder resets the memo whenever files change). The
    binding.source_file back-fill side effect moves with it.
    
    * perf(git): reuse per-thread Repo handles; batch the update commit walk
    
    Isolated measurement showed the git phase cost was NOT the repo-wide
    walks (commit index 1.1s, co-change 0.4s, prior-defects 0.4s on a 1.9k
    file repo) but constructing a fresh gitpython.Repo per FILE in the
    worker threads (~40ms each, ~64s serial). Reuse one Repo per worker
    thread via a small pool (closed when the index pass completes):
    index_repo FULL drops 101.6s -> 18.4s isolated (ESSENTIAL 77.7s -> 3.1s).
    
    index_changed_files (the update path) now also reads from the batched
    repo-wide commit index instead of spawning one `git log -- <file>` per
    changed file, making update metadata identical to a fresh full index --
    including the agent-provenance rollup, which the per-file fallback never
    classified (updates silently zeroed agent_commit_count/agent_authored_pct
    for changed files).
    
    Equivalence-tested: update-path metadata == full-index metadata per file.
    
    * perf(generation): batch RAG prefetch for level-2 file pages
    
    Each tier-1 file page used to fire its own vector_store.search() inside
    the LLM semaphore -- one embedder round-trip (50-200ms network) per page,
    serialized against generation slots, plus a per-page list_page_ids()
    store-size probe.
    
    Add VectorStore.search_many (default: concurrent per-query gather;
    InMemory/LanceDB/PgVector override to embed ALL queries in one embedder
    call -- N network round-trips become 1) and resolve RAG context for the
    whole level in one prefetch before level 2 starts, outside the semaphore.
    The store is static for the level (pages embed in one batch at level
    end), so prefetched results are identical to per-page fetches. On batch
    failure pages fall back to the unchanged per-page path.
    
    * perf(persistence): batch pgvector embed UPDATEs + IN-filtered summary lookups
    
    - PgVectorStore.embed_batch: pass the param list to one execute()
      (executemany) instead of looping one UPDATE round-trip per row.
    - PgVectorStore.get_page_summaries_by_paths: one parameterized
      expanding-IN SELECT instead of the base-class per-path gather
      (N queries -> 1 for the level-2 dependency-summary prefetch).
    - LanceDBVectorStore.get_page_summaries_by_paths: one IN-filtered scan
      with the same quote-doubling escape the single-path lookup uses.
    
    Both overrides keep the single-path semantics: first row per path wins,
    empty summaries dropped.
    
    * perf(generation): index symbol nodes once for call-graph/heritage extraction
    
    extract_call_graph and extract_heritage each scanned EVERY graph node per
    file, and level-2 context assembly runs them for every code file --
    O(files x nodes) per generation run (8s+ at 2k files / 30k symbol nodes,
    growing quadratically with repo size). Build a file_path -> symbol-nodes
    index in one pass and share it across the level (8s -> 0.27s measured on
    a synthetic 2k-file graph).
    
    Buckets preserve graph node iteration order, so extraction results are
    byte-identical to the scan (equivalence-tested); the scan path remains
    the default for single-file callers.
    
    * style: fix lint in new phase-2 files (docstring glyph, import order)
    
    * perf(graph): parallelize exact betweenness centrality over a process pool
    
    nx.betweenness_centrality is single-threaded pure-Python Brandes; on the
    ~11k-node symbol subgraph it was 54s isolated - the single largest block
    of both init's graph.metrics phase and every incremental update. Brandes
    is embarrassingly parallel over source nodes, so fan the source set out
    across a ProcessPoolExecutor (attribute-free int-relabeled edge list per
    worker) and sum the partials. Values are identical to the sequential
    call modulo float summation order (equivalence-tested at rel 1e-9).
    
    Small graphs and any pool failure fall back to the sequential NetworkX
    path. Measured: symbol betweenness 54.0s -> 8.3s isolated.
    
    * perf(dead-code): compile never-flag globs into one alternation regex
    
    _should_never_flag ran fnmatch.fnmatch per (node x pattern) - ~540
    patterns x 27k calls = 14.6M fnmatch invocations, each normcasing both
    sides (29M normcase calls; LCMapStringEx dominates on Windows). Profiled
    at 50.7s of a 51.5s dead-code analyze() on the repowise graph.
    
    Pre-normcase and translate each glob once, join into a single compiled
    alternation, and match the normcased path - byte-for-byte the same
    semantics fnmatch implements internally (equivalence-tested per glob
    and against a probe-path corpus). Measured: dead-code pass 20.7s ->
    3.7s isolated.
    
    * perf(persistence): replace per-item SELECTs in batch upserts with one keyed query
    
    The generic _batch_upsert issued a SELECT round-trip per item; persisting
    13k graph nodes meant 13k SELECTs (9.3s isolated), and the same pattern
    taxed edges (31k), symbols (11k), git metadata, commits, and function
    blame on every init and update. The keyed variant fetches all candidate
    rows for the repo once, matches natural keys in memory, and keeps the
    ORM update/insert + flush semantics (within-batch duplicate keys still
    converge to one row, equivalent to the old autoflush-visible behavior).
    
    All seven callers converted; the per-item loop had no remaining users
    and is removed. Measured: persist_graph_nodes 9.3s -> 2.5s isolated.
    
    * perf(parser): execute the compiled tree-sitter query once per file
    
    parse_file ran cursor.matches() over the whole tree five times - once
    each for symbols, imports, calls, heritage, and type refs - against the
    same compiled query. The plan's 3.2 ambiguity resolves to the contained
    case: one query, re-iterated; no .scm changes needed. Materialize the
    capture list once and route it through all five extraction passes
    (read-only consumers, verified). extract_heritage drops its tree/query/
    run_query params for the shared match list.
    
    Output is identical by construction (same capture dicts in the same
    order); the per-language fixture suites pin correctness and a new guard
    test asserts the query executes exactly once per file. Measured:
    sequential full-repo parse 4.7s -> 2.7s.
    
    * perf(health): cache duplication token streams + windows by content hash
    
    The duplication pass re-tokenized every file (a second tree-sitter parse
    plus a pure-Python leaf walk) and re-rolled 1.2M window hashes on every
    run - ~11s isolated on the repowise repo, paid in full by incremental
    updates that changed one file. Both outputs are pure functions of the
    file bytes given a fixed window size, so cache them per content hash in
    .repowise/duplication_cache.pkl: unchanged files replay their cached
    kind sequence + window tuples (WindowHash is rebuilt with the current
    path, making hits rename-proof); changed files tokenize as before.
    
    The minified/token-cap/window-budget gates stay live against cached
    lengths, the verifier now compares per-file kind lists (the only Token
    field it ever read), and any cache error degrades to a full re-tokenize.
    Cache equivalence, hit/miss/invalidation, and corrupt-file fallback are
    unit-tested; wired through HealthAnalyzer for init, update, rescore, and
    the health CLI.

commit bd1f1f1d3f27d0cb47c6fe73e3876520f45e2a5f
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Thu Jun 4 20:26:41 2026 +0530

    feat(git): deterministic agent-provenance layer in the git indexer (#366)
    
    Classify every walked commit {agent, autonomy_tier, channel, confidence}
    from local-git attribution channels (bot/service identities, message
    footers, co-author trailers), riding the existing commit-index walk with
    no extra git pass (~20 us/commit). Persist per-commit columns on
    git_commits and per-file rollups (agent_commit_count, agent_authored_pct,
    agent_tier_counts_json) on git_metadata, with an Alembic migration.
    
    Patterns are precision-first (anchored to service identities, never bare
    names; service committer over a human author is tier 2, not 1) and
    config-extensible via an agent_provenance block in .repowise/config.yaml.
    Surfaced in the /git-metadata file view, the wiki At-a-glance panel, and
    the MCP get_context ownership block.

commit d30c3752085cbb63921f4356f60f00f28edbabce
Author: Swati Ahuja <swatiahuja.ahuja@gmail.com>
Date:   Thu Jun 4 17:46:37 2026 +0530

    feat(cli): owl mascot init banner with repo-seeded heatmap wordmark (#364)
    
    * feat(cli): owl mascot banner with repo-seeded heatmap wordmark
    
    New ui/mascot.py: owl art, REPOWISE block wordmark (full + compact),
    GitHub-heatmap colouring seeded from the repo name (zlib.crc32 — stable
    across processes), and owl spinner frames registered with rich.
    
    print_banner keeps its signature but now renders the owl banner,
    width-adaptive: full design >= 82 cols, same design at 1-char strokes
    below. Facade gains banner_text/mini/OWL_SPINNER/THINKING_FRAMES
    (additive only).
    
    * feat(cli): owl spinner everywhere + completion/interrupt mascot moments
    
    All 10 spinner sites (4 SpinnerColumn, 6 console.status) now use
    OWL_SPINNER — the eye-roll owl frames, falling back to "dots" if rich's
    private spinner registry ever moves. Completion panels get a happy owl
    {^ ^} in the title; the Ctrl-C interrupt message a sleepy {─ ─}.
    Provider/validation errors stay sober on purpose.
    
    * fix(cli): review fixes — escape repo name, brand status spinners, derive width threshold
    
    - print_banner escapes repo_name before markup interpolation: a directory
      name containing a closing-tag sequence (e.g. 'evil[/bold]name') crashed
      with MarkupError; now renders literally (pre-existing, exposed by review)
    - the 6 console.status sites pass spinner_style=BRAND_STYLE so the owl is
      brand orange everywhere, matching the SpinnerColumn sites
    - full/compact switch threshold now derives from mascot.banner_width()
      (+4-col margin) instead of a hardcoded 82 in brand.py — font changes
      can't silently desync the layout; width pinned by a new test
    
    * polish(cli): nitpick fixes from review
    
    - status spinners back to rich's default green (owl frames kept) — the
      orange was only ever specified for the Progress columns
    - happy-owl prefix moves into build_completion_panel so future panels
      can't forget it; reporting.py reverts to plain titles
    - drop _LOGO: hand-pasted duplicate of art mascot.py owns (drift risk),
      and nothing reads it
    - seed-parity test rewritten behaviorally: asserts rendered span colors
      equal heat_grid(seed_for(repo)) predictions for both sizes, instead of
      monkeypatch-spying on seed_for
    - EYES_ERROR documented as reserved (errors stay sober by design)

commit 08cf155c5b43742eca351cc870c183efb05730e4
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Thu Jun 4 17:45:14 2026 +0530

    fix(generation): close never-started page coroutines on cancellation (#365)
    
    Ctrl+C during generation cancels the run_level gather; pages still
    queued on the concurrency semaphore never start their inner coroutine.
    CancelledError is a BaseException, so it bypassed the except-Exception
    handler in guarded_named and the coroutine objects leaked, spraying one
    "coroutine ... was never awaited" RuntimeWarning per pending page at
    interpreter shutdown (~980 with default concurrency on a 985-page run).
    
    Add a BaseException handler that closes the inner coroutine before
    re-raising. close() is a no-op on a coroutine that already ran, so the
    in-flight page is unaffected. No behaviour change beyond silencing the
    teardown warnings - completed pages were already persisted and --resume
    already picked up cleanly.
    
    Fixes the RuntimeWarning reported in #358.

commit dafde049092267cb696e64cf1704e812df95d2b0
Author: joptimus <jlewan27@gmail.com>
Date:   Thu Jun 4 06:57:05 2026 -0500

    fix(server): honor exclude_patterns in server jobs; prune stale rows (#354)
    
    Server-triggered jobs (web sync/full-resync, workspace sync, webhooks,
    scheduler) ran the shared pipeline without exclude_patterns, so excluded
    paths kept getting re-indexed even when set in repo settings or
    .repowise/config.yaml. execute_job() now resolves excludes from both
    sources and passes them into run_pipeline().
    
    Full pipeline persistence also did not remove rows for files that
    disappeared from the latest result, so a directory newly added to
    exclude_patterns left stale graph/dead-code/health/git data behind.
    persist_pipeline_result() now prunes file-scoped rows before re-upserting,
    using parsed_files as the source of truth for graph/analysis tables and
    git_metadata_list for git_metadata (a file can be git-tracked but unparsed).

commit 61b84e29191b4a1154d35ee160b5310031a613ed
Author: joptimus <jlewan27@gmail.com>
Date:   Thu Jun 4 06:46:11 2026 -0500

    fix(web): decode breadcrumb path labels (#359)

commit b6bbe1f8856ca004bbc7214b093689fcb3e29dc7
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Thu Jun 4 16:47:55 2026 +0530

    fix(health): calibrate hotspot + ownership signals for small teams and quiet repos (#361) (#363)
    
    Hotspot classification was purely repo-relative (top-quartile decayed churn
    + any 90d activity), so on a quiet repo a single drive-by maintenance commit
    was enough to flag a file as a hotspot - inflating the hotspot table, the
    hotspot-health KPI, and the HIGH->CRITICAL severity escalations that key off
    the flag. Ownership signals had no team-size awareness, so 1-3 person repos
    saw bus-factor/concentration warnings for what is simply their normal
    operating model.
    
    Three changes:
    
    1. Absolute activity floors on is_hotspot (both classification paths -
       enrich.compute_percentiles and the SQL PERCENT_RANK recompute): a hotspot
       now also needs >= 3 commits in 90d AND real line movement
       (temporal_hotspot_score >= 0.5, with a >= 8-commits escape hatch for
       files whose numstat carries no line counts).
    
    2. Repo-level active-contributor count (90d, bot-filtered, anchored to the
       repo's own most recent activity so historical checkouts stay correct),
       computed once per health run from per-author timestamps already in
       top_authors_json and exposed on FileContext.
    
    3. Small-team severity gating: with <= 3 active contributors,
       concentration-only ownership_risk / knowledge_loss findings are capped at
       LOW (kept, annotated as informational) unless corroborated by hotspot
       activity; the get_risk bus-factor-risk label gets the same gate. Unknown
       team size (pre-timestamp indexes) keeps historical behaviour.
    
    Validated against the 21-repo / 9-language defect benchmark at T0
    (t0_floor_experiment.py, re-scoring cached findings through the product
    scorer with the demoted-flag transformations): corpus pooled AUC
    0.7319 -> 0.7334 (keyword labels; bootstrap CI [-0.0022, +0.0045]) and
    0.7446 -> 0.7473 under SZZ labels (CI [+0.0003, +0.0049] - excludes zero),
    mean Popt up on both. On quiet repos the floors demote ~70% of flags, all
    with 1-2 drive-by commits; the small-team gate touches no corpus repo
    (all have > 3 active contributors).

commit 413e82905e2d48b8699c7a61c2b98920c2fc555a
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Wed Jun 3 22:05:53 2026 +0530

    feat(plugin): Claude Code plugin + root marketplace, refreshed to current surface (#356)
    
    Distribute the Claude Code plugin through a marketplace manifest at the repo
    root and bring it up to date with the shipped product.
    
    - Add .claude-plugin/marketplace.json (source: ./plugins/claude-code) so users
      install with `/plugin marketplace add repowise-dev/repowise`.
    - Correct the MCP surface to the nine exposed tools; drop references to the
      unexposed get_dependency_path / get_architecture_diagram.
    - Add commands: health, risk, dead-code, decision, doctor.
    - Add skills: code-health and change-review; refresh the existing four.
    - Bundle a PostToolUse hook (repowise-augment) and de-duplicate emissions so
      the bundled hook and the init-installed settings.json hook never double-fire.
    - Document the fifth layer (Code Health) across commands and docs.
    - README + Quickstart: add the plugin install path and fix stale
      PreToolUse/PostToolUse wording (hooks are PostToolUse-only).

commit 4a280c4b7eb0ecf547f3a2b899dbd2e588bd5040
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Wed Jun 3 17:34:36 2026 +0530

    release: v0.16.0 (#350)

commit a57d7958aa5576adae9f98ec2e9e5e0dbace7a6f
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Wed Jun 3 17:22:49 2026 +0530

    fix(git): make "last touched" the contributor's own last commit + honor .mailmap (#349)
    
    Owner "last touched" showed a file's last commit by *anyone* among files the
    person co-owns, so a teammate's commit to a shared file bumped your timestamp.
    Two fixes:
    
    - Record each author's own first/last commit timestamp in top_authors_json and
      aggregate "last touched" / "first commit" from that, falling back to the
      file-level value for indexes built before the field existed.
    - Read author identity through git's mailmap (%aN/%aE instead of %an/%ae) so a
      repo's .mailmap folds one person's multiple names/emails (personal vs. GitHub
      noreply, second machine) into a single contributor bucket.
    
    Adds a .mailmap coalescing the duplicated identities in this repo.

commit 2e21dad38c7ea4c2da00d0191b0a62c1eeed2b96
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Wed Jun 3 14:36:15 2026 +0530

    Add Codex CLI provider and project integration (#348)
    
    * feat: add Codex CLI provider and project integration (#195)
    
    Add a local Codex CLI LLM provider built on authenticated `codex exec`
    sessions: argv-based subprocess (no shell), JSONL parsing tolerant of
    non-JSON noise, an async semaphore, a 600s exec timeout with kill, reasoning
    mapped to `model_reasoning_effort`, and zero-cost subscription usage tracking
    (flagged `estimated` when Codex omits usage).
    
    Add project-local Codex setup: `.codex/config.toml` MCP server, `.codex`
    lifecycle hooks (SessionStart / UserPromptSubmit / PostToolUse) installed via
    the import-isolated `repowise-augment` entry point, `.codex-plugin` metadata,
    skills, a plugin marketplace entry, and managed `AGENTS.md` generation reusing
    the existing marker-merge generator.
    
    Wire reasoning across all LLM providers (base interface + per-provider model
    discovery and supported reasoning modes) and add a richer interactive
    provider / model / reasoning selection flow to `repowise init`, with new
    `--codex/--no-codex` and `--agents/--no-agents` flags. Codex setup is opt-in:
    interactive runs prompt (default no); non-interactive runs require `--codex`.
    
    Squashes the six commits from #195 and rebases them onto current main,
    resolving conflicts from the init_cmd/ and ui/ package splits, the
    editor-setup integration abstraction, and the provider cost-tracker
    inline-await change:
      - feat: add Codex editor integration
      - docs: document Codex setup
      - Reasoning work
      - Add provider model discovery to init
      - Accept cache hints in Codex provider
      - Close Codex subprocess transport
    
    * fix(codex): address review — event-loop blocking, TOML safety, hook gating
    
    Follow-up fixes from a post-merge review of the Codex integration:
    
    - Run the Codex `_build_command` reasoning-catalog load and Gemini's lazy
      model discovery via `asyncio.to_thread`, so a cold `codex debug models`
      call or a paginated Gemini `/models` fetch can't stall every concurrent
      generation coroutine on the event loop.
    - Re-validate the merged `.codex/config.toml` with `tomllib` before writing
      in `save_codex_mcp_config` / `enable_codex_hooks_feature`. The table-rewrite
      regex only matches the bare key spelling; a quoted or inline `repowise`/
      `features` entry would otherwise append a duplicate-key block and corrupt
      the user's file. Now it aborts cleanly, original file untouched.
    - Gate the edit-tool PostToolUse freshness notice on `client == "codex"` so a
      future widening of the Claude installer's matcher can't emit Codex banners
      to existing Claude Code users. Adds a regression test.
    
    ---------
    
    Co-authored-by: Selene29 <funmailfach@web.de>

commit b0c8954031ec98eece57bdd595f8fa6ef90414db
Author: joptimus <jlewan27@gmail.com>
Date:   Wed Jun 3 02:40:10 2026 -0500

    feat(cli): add advisory update check to doctor (#346)
    
    Surface when the installed repowise CLI is out of date and the exact
    command to update it, without ever auto-updating the user's environment.
    
    - Add `repowise.cli.update_check`: a conservative, never-raising helper
      that reads the current version, queries the PyPI JSON endpoint via the
      bundled httpx (short timeout), and suggests an install-method-aware
      upgrade command (uv tool / pipx / pip / editable checkout).
    - Add a `CLI version` row to `repowise doctor`: advisory only — it shows
      current vs latest plus both the resolved PATH executable and the full
      running command (these can differ), never flips doctor's pass/fail, and
      swallows network errors. Reminds the user to restart their MCP client
      when an update is available.
    - Treat an unparsable PyPI version as "unknown" rather than "up to date".
    - Document the check in CLI_REFERENCE.md and USER_GUIDE.md.
    
    Closes #338

commit bbb568d87c8b5e0b046e6e016d20b0f90b4dab67
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Tue Jun 2 22:48:37 2026 +0530

    docs: fix broken README and CONTRIBUTING links (#345)
    
    The repowise-bench repo uses `master` as its default branch, but the README linked its reports via /blob/main/, producing four 404s (COMPARISON_REPORT, BENCHMARK_REPORT, flask48, sklearn48). Point them at /blob/master/. Also fix the root CONTRIBUTING link to its actual path under .github/.
    
    Refresh CONTRIBUTING.md while here: use the workspace-correct `uv sync --all-packages`, run tests via `uv run pytest`, list the types and ui packages, fix the LICENSE link, and add Conventional Commits and language/provider-recipe pointers.

commit 034876b053cf70b889979d2552c6a2bf9763df37
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Tue Jun 2 22:34:38 2026 +0530

    refactor(cli): split init_cmd into a structured package (#344)
    
    Break the 2k-line init_cmd.py into a cohesive init_cmd/ package —
    command, workspace, generation, persistence, reporting, and the
    interactive prompts — and collapse the duplicated single-repo and
    per-repo workspace generation paths into one shared core (coverage
    selection, cost gate, generation + KG enrichment + cost flush).
    
    Also replace the two hand-rolled config.yaml yaml.dump writes with
    save_config_partial (extended to take arbitrary keys), and route the
    DB-engine setup through the existing open_repo_db helper.
    
    The public import surface is preserved: init_command plus the
    leading-underscore names sibling commands (search/reindex) and the
    test-suite import from repowise.cli.commands.init_cmd still resolve.

commit f433e4c970301829d57f9cd10184f48768bf056e
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Tue Jun 2 21:40:51 2026 +0530

    feat(pipeline): make `repowise init --resume` actually resume (#343)
    
    * refactor(pipeline): split persist_pipeline_result into per-phase persisters
    
    Decompose the monolithic persist recipe into four independently-callable,
    phase-scoped functions — persist_ingestion (graph nodes/edges, external
    systems, symbols, security), persist_git (metadata, commits),
    persist_analysis (dead code, health, decisions, governance), and
    persist_generation (pages, KG). persist_pipeline_result now just composes
    them in dependency order.
    
    This is the backbone for incremental, resumable persistence: the
    orchestrator can persist a single phase's outputs the moment it completes
    instead of holding everything in memory until one terminal write. Public
    API (persist_pipeline_result, persist_graph_nodes) is unchanged and the
    end-state is identical — verified against the persistence + pipeline
    integration suites (235 tests green).
    
    * fix(init): persist + restore git tier across resume; honor fast mode in workspaces
    
    Two related fixes so `--resume` and multi-repo init respect the fast/index
    tier instead of silently doing more work than asked:
    
    - Record `run_mode`/`git_tier` in state.json on every init (both index-only
      and full paths). On `--resume`, continue the prior run's tier via
      `_effective_run_mode_for_resume` so a resumed fast index no longer falls
      back to STANDARD and redoes the FULL git indexing (per-file blame +
      co-change) the first run deliberately skipped. An explicit `--mode fast`
      on the resume still wins.
    - Thread `run_mode` through `_workspace_init` → `_WorkspaceCtx` → the
      per-repo `run_pipeline` call, mapping it to `OrchestratorMode.FAST`.
      Previously the multi-repo flow never passed `mode=`, so `--mode fast` was
      silently ignored for workspaces.
    
    Adds unit coverage for the resume tier-restore logic.
    
    * feat(pipeline): add resume building blocks — ledger, rehydration, controller
    
    Introduce a modular `pipeline/resume/` package, the foundation for skipping
    already-completed work on `repowise init --resume`. Nothing imports it yet,
    so this is behaviour-neutral; the orchestrator/CLI wiring follows.
    
    - phases.py: coarse checkpoint vocabulary (INDEX → ANALYSIS → GENERATION)
      chosen for resume value; INDEX bundles the expensive git history +
      graph centrality so a health-phase interrupt resumes cheaply.
    - ledger.py: ResumeLedger — durable "which phases completed" record over the
      existing pipeline_jobs table / SqlJobStore. Each write commits its own
      session so a completion survives the process being killed. Best-effort.
    - rehydrate.py: rehydrate_git_meta_map rebuilds git_meta_map from persisted
      rows via column introspection (graph side reuses rehydrate_graph_builder).
      Documents that the transient BlameIndex is intentionally not persisted.
    - controller.py: ResumeController — the single object the orchestrator will
      consult at phase boundaries (can_skip with a prefix rule, checkpoint_index,
      rehydrate_index). Builds its ledger from the session factory; requires a
      real Repository.id (fixes the old str(repo_path) FK violation).
    
    Unit-tested against in-memory SQLite: ledger lifecycle + cross-process
    visibility, git_meta_map round-trip, and the controller skip/prefix logic.
    
    * feat(pipeline): wire resume fast-path into the orchestrator
    
    run_pipeline now accepts an opt-in ResumeController. When a prior run
    persisted the INDEX phase, the resumed run rehydrates the graph + git
    metadata from the DB and only re-parses source (new parse-only
    `reparse_for_resume`), skipping the git-history walk and the four graph
    centrality kernels — the minutes-long work behind the "safe to Ctrl-C,
    then --resume" message. When not resuming, the freshly-built index is
    checkpointed right after external-systems extraction so an interrupt
    during analysis can resume.
    
    Strictly opt-in: with resume_controller=None (every current caller) the
    gather path and behaviour are byte-identical. Verified by a new
    integration test that runs the real pipeline twice on the sample repo and
    asserts the resumed run never calls _run_ingestion / _run_git_indexing yet
    still produces a rehydrated graph + re-parsed files.
    
    * feat(init): wire resume controller into `repowise init`
    
    `repowise init` now creates the repository row + engine before the pipeline
    (in the same event loop) and passes a ResumeController, so the index is
    checkpointed to the DB the moment it's built. An interrupt during analysis
    now leaves a resumable, persisted index; `repowise init --resume`
    rehydrates the graph + git metadata and only re-parses source, skipping the
    git-history walk and graph centrality — making the long-standing "safe to
    Ctrl-C, then --resume" message true.
    
    Details:
    - ResumeController tracks whether the INDEX was *actually* persisted
      (checkpoint success) or rehydrated; PipelineResult.index_persisted_
      incrementally reflects that. The final persist then writes only analysis +
      generation when the index is already on disk — avoiding a redundant write
      and never re-persisting a rehydrated graph. A best-effort checkpoint
      failure leaves the flag False, so the full persist still runs (no data
      loss).
    - Repository row created up front fixes the pipeline_jobs.repository_id FK
      (was str(repo_path), now the real Repository.id).
    - Dry runs skip all of this — they must not touch the database.
    
    Verified: full CLI integration suite (16) green, including dry-run and
    init-twice; resume integration + unit suites green.
    
    * feat(init): persist generated pages incrementally + reuse prior pages on resume
    
    Generation buffered every page in memory and only wrote them to the DB once,
    at the very end of the run. An interrupt mid-generation therefore lost the
    whole batch: pages already embedded into the vector store are skipped on the
    next resume (the store is the resume ground truth) yet were never written to
    the pages table, so the wiki stayed permanently incomplete.
    
    Wrap init's generation in run_generation_with_persistence, which:
    - loads persisted pages up front and hands them to the generator as
      prior_pages, so unchanged pages skip the LLM call (same reuse as update);
    - flushes each page to the DB the instant it is generated via the on_page_ready
      sink, on a dedicated engine (the sole wiki.db writer during generation, since
      cost rows are buffered until afterwards), so an interrupt leaves a usable
      partial wiki the next resume reuses.
    
    upsert_page is now a no-op when content, source_hash and model are unchanged,
    so the end-of-run persist of the same pages neither bumps the version nor
    archives a redundant PageVersion.
    
    * feat(init): make long synchronous index phases interruptible with Ctrl-C
    
    The #341 repro had two halves: the duplication-detector hang (fixed in #342)
    and 'Ctrl-C didn't return the terminal.' The latter is structural — heavy CPU
    phases run in asyncio.to_thread workers, and a bare SIGINT frees the event
    loop's main thread while the non-daemon worker keeps grinding, so the process
    never exits.
    
    Add cooperative cancellation: a process-global CancellationToken, armed for the
    run by a cancellation_scope() context manager in 'repowise init'. The first
    Ctrl-C flips the token (and the long duplication loops, which now poll
    check_cancelled() once per file / per hash bucket, unwind promptly); a second
    forces a hard quit. The token's PipelineCancelled subclasses BaseException so
    the pipeline's broad 'except Exception' phase guards can't swallow it — it
    always propagates to the CLI, which prints a 'run --resume' hint and exits.
    
    The interrupted run keeps its INDEX checkpoint on disk, so --resume reuses the
    expensive graph + git index rather than rebuilding it.
    
    * feat(pipeline): skip analysis recompute on a generation-interrupted resume
    
    Resume previously skipped only the INDEX phase; a run interrupted during the
    long generation phase still re-ran dead-code, health and (the costly,
    LLM-backed) decision extraction on resume.
    
    Checkpoint ANALYSIS mid-run — after decision extraction, before generation —
    via ResumeController.checkpoint_analysis, mirroring the existing INDEX
    checkpoint, so a generation interrupt leaves ANALYSIS marked complete. On
    resume, can_skip(ANALYSIS) rehydrates thin dead-code + decision reports
    (rehydrate_dead_code_report / rehydrate_decision_report) as generation input
    only and skips the recompute entirely.
    
    The rehydrated views carry just the fields the page generator reads and are
    never written back: the persisted analysis rows are authoritative, and
    persist_analysis (guarded on truthy reports) no-ops on the result's None
    reports while still folding harvested decisions + governance. Falls back to a
    full recompute if rehydration errors — the default (no controller) path is
    unchanged.
    
    * fix(init): forward repo_path to run_generation in the persistence wrapper
    
    run_generation_with_persistence consumed repo_path as its own parameter
    (to build the incremental-flush engine) but never forwarded it to
    run_generation, which requires it keyword-only. Every full (non
    index-only) init crashed at the generation phase with a missing-argument
    TypeError; index-only runs skip the wrapper and were unaffected.
    
    The unit fakes accepted **_kw, silently swallowing the unforwarded
    argument and hiding the broken contract. Harden all three to take
    repo_path as a required parameter and assert it, so a regression fails
    the test.
    
    * feat(ingestion): honour nested .gitignore files, not just the repo root
    
    FileTraverser only loaded the repo-root .gitignore; nested .gitignore
    files in subdirectories were ignored. In a monorepo or yarn/npm
    workspace, a package keeping its own .gitignore (excluding its build
    output, generated bundles, coverage, etc.) was not respected, so that
    generated content got pulled into the index.
    
    Git reads a .gitignore in every directory, not just the root. The
    per-directory ignore machinery already existed for .repowiseIgnore, so
    _get_dir_ignore now merges each directory's nested .gitignore and
    .repowiseIgnore into one spec. The root stays handled by the existing
    full-path self._gitignore / self._extra_ignore (no double-reads), and
    matching semantics are unchanged from the existing per-directory
    .repowiseIgnore handling.
    
    Also documents .gitignore / .repowiseIgnore / exclude precedence in
    docs/CONFIG.md. Adds TestNestedGitignore (4 cases).

commit 8cad94696a992500300565eb29926eea2e773c88
Author: joptimus <jlewan27@gmail.com>
Date:   Tue Jun 2 09:50:10 2026 -0500

    fix(update): re-run health on config change even when git is unchanged (#337)
    
    * fix(update): re-run health on config change even when git is unchanged
    
    `repowise update` only re-scored health for files that changed in git, so
    editing `.repowise/config.yaml` (exclude_patterns) or `health-rules.json` had no
    effect until a code change happened to touch each file. Issue 3 of #296.
    
    - Add `config_fingerprint(repo_path)` (sha256 of config.yaml + health-rules.json)
      to helpers; `repowise init` stores it in state after the config is written so
      the first update does not false-positive.
    - `repowise update` compares the stored fingerprint to the current one. A real
      change triggers `_run_full_health_rescore`: rebuild the graph with current
      exclude_patterns, load GitMetadata from the DB (so biomarkers keep accurate
      churn/ownership data, including the newer prior_defect_count / change_entropy
      columns), drop excluded git rows, full-replace health metrics/findings, and
      refresh graph nodes. A missing prior fingerprint is backfilled, not treated as
      a change.
    - The config-change path takes precedence over the partial update (so unchanged
      files are re-scored under the new rules), is skipped under `--dry-run`, and
      does not advance the fingerprint if the re-score fails (so it retries).
    
    Tests: config_fingerprint, _git_metadata_to_dict, a failed-rescore-keeps-old-
    fingerprint unit test, and integration tests covering init storage, the
    unchanged/changed/dry-run paths, and config-change-with-source-diffs.
    
    Closes #296 (issue 3)
    
    * refactor(update): share rebuild path between incremental update and health re-score
    
    Maintainer feedback on #337:
    
    - Extract the traverse/parse/graph-build (+ framework-aware synthetic edges)
      into a shared _build_repo_graph helper, used by both _rebuild_graph_and_git
      and _run_full_health_rescore — instead of a second graph-rebuild copy inside
      the re-score. The config re-score now includes the framework-edge step it
      was previously missing, so both paths build the same graph. (Not routed
      through _persist_index_only: config re-score needs full health replacement,
      not partial upsert.)
    - Drop the unused cfg parameter from _run_full_health_rescore (git metadata is
      loaded from the DB, not re-indexed, so commit_limit/follow_renames are moot).
    - Replace the silent 'except Exception: pass' parse swallow with a skipped-file
      count warning in the shared helper.
    
    Adds TestBuildRepoGraph covering the parse-skip warning and the framework-edge
    step; updates the existing re-score test for the new signature.

commit 5ecff5aa00a4f4788df197acf2f9e5a3bb3f7ffa
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Tue Jun 2 18:00:51 2026 +0530

    fix(health): bound duplication detection so minified bundles can't wedge `init` (#342)
    
    Clone detection tokenized every file and compared all window pairs within
    each hash bucket — an O(k^2) loop guarded only by the assumption that
    "buckets are small in practice." Minified/generated bundles (e.g. a
    checked-in `storybook-static/` chunk) collapse into hundreds of thousands
    of near-identical windows, so one bucket explodes into billions of
    comparisons. Because this runs before the per-file progress loop, `init`
    sat at `health 0/N` indefinitely (#341).
    
    Add layered, independent resource guards in a new `duplication/limits.py`:
    
    - skip minified/generated files via a cheap pre-tokenize byte scan
      (average / longest line length);
    - cap per-file tokens and the repo-wide window budget;
    - skip degenerate hash buckets larger than a threshold (the direct
      O(k^2) cap — oversized buckets are boilerplate noise, never real
      clones);
    - a soft wall-clock deadline as the final backstop.
    
    Any single layer suffices, so no repo shape can wedge the run again.
    `detect_clones` is refactored into bounded named stages, and the engine
    logs `health_duplication_limits` when a guard fires so skipped bundles
    are no longer invisible.

commit 8f9fc1ba5837af59f2e216c511549f19d5773bbc
Author: joptimus <jlewan27@gmail.com>
Date:   Tue Jun 2 05:26:55 2026 -0500

    fix(mcp): exclude_patterns filtering for context, answer, and federated search (PR 4b) (#340)
    
    * fix(mcp): filter exclude_patterns from MCP tool responses
    
    Excluded files are skipped at ingest time, but rows may predate an
    exclude_patterns change, so MCP tools now also filter their results against the
    repo's exclude_patterns at query time. Issue 5 of #296.
    
    Adds per-repo filter helpers to _helpers.py (a compiled PathSpec from the repo
    config plus shape-specific filters: ORM rows by attribute, GraphNode file vs
    symbol nodes, result dicts by key, path lists, and "path::Name" node ids), each
    covered by parametrized tests, then wires them into the structured tools:
    
    - health, overview, dead_code, community, metrics, callers, symbol, diagram,
      dependency, risk, flows, why, decision_records
    - search (single-repo path)
    
    Single-file/target tools (annotate, dependency, metrics, callers, symbol, why,
    risk) reject or omit excluded targets; list/aggregate tools drop excluded rows
    before KPIs/rollups so excluded files never affect the numbers.
    
    Includes an end-to-end test proving get_health honors exclude_patterns from
    .repowise/config.yaml.
    
    Out of scope (follow-up): tool_context enrichment, tool_answer retrieval, and the
    federated ("all repos") search path, which need filtering inside their
    context/retrieval pipelines rather than a result-level helper call.
    
    Part of #296 (issue 5)
    
    * fix(mcp): filter exclude_patterns in context, answer, and federated search
    
    PR 4b — the custom-logic MCP surfaces deferred from PR 4a (#339), where
    filtering must happen inside each pipeline rather than at a single result list.
    
    - get_context: gate excluded file/symbol-id targets; filter imported_by,
      used_by, module child files, candidates, callers/callees, community
      members, and fuzzy not-found suggestions.
    - get_answer: drop excluded hits after hydration and after 1-hop graph
      expansion; bypass a cached answer to re-synthesis when it references a
      now-excluded path (in its fields or prose).
    - search (federated): attach target_path inside _search_single_repo and
      filter each repo by its own exclude_patterns before RRF aggregation.
    
    Adds tests for all three surfaces plus the cache-bypass and suggestion
    regressions.

commit 1fd50a5a663805cc28368cee0a07daf70cc22995
Author: joptimus <jlewan27@gmail.com>
Date:   Tue Jun 2 05:26:07 2026 -0500

    fix(mcp): filter exclude_patterns from MCP tool responses (#339)
    
    Excluded files are skipped at ingest time, but rows may predate an
    exclude_patterns change, so MCP tools now also filter their results against the
    repo's exclude_patterns at query time. Issue 5 of #296.
    
    Adds per-repo filter helpers to _helpers.py (a compiled PathSpec from the repo
    config plus shape-specific filters: ORM rows by attribute, GraphNode file vs
    symbol nodes, result dicts by key, path lists, and "path::Name" node ids), each
    covered by parametrized tests, then wires them into the structured tools:
    
    - health, overview, dead_code, community, metrics, callers, symbol, diagram,
      dependency, risk, flows, why, decision_records
    - search (single-repo path)
    
    Single-file/target tools (annotate, dependency, metrics, callers, symbol, why,
    risk) reject or omit excluded targets; list/aggregate tools drop excluded rows
    before KPIs/rollups so excluded files never affect the numbers.
    
    Includes an end-to-end test proving get_health honors exclude_patterns from
    .repowise/config.yaml.
    
    Out of scope (follow-up): tool_context enrichment, tool_answer retrieval, and the
    federated ("all repos") search path, which need filtering inside their
    context/retrieval pipelines rather than a result-level helper call.
    
    Part of #296 (issue 5)

commit 24ab62156b4d941c9c6224728d838c82d17e01af
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jun 1 23:47:26 2026 +0530

    fix(mcp): deep-merge per-server entry so user-added `env` survives re-registration (#336)
    
    `merge_mcp_entry()` and `save_root_mcp_config()` did a shallow
    `servers.update(new_entry)`, which replaced the entire `repowise` server
    value. Since `generate_mcp_config()` emits no `env` key, any user-added
    `env` block (BYOK provider/embedder keys for the stdio server) was
    silently wiped on every re-registration — `repowise init`, `update`, and
    anything calling `register_with_claude_*`. That dropped the configured
    embedder back to a mock and quietly degraded semantic search.
    
    Merge each server definition into the existing dict instead: generated
    `command`/`args`/`description` are refreshed, but other keys the user
    added (notably `env`) are preserved.
    
    Fixes #307.

commit b6b7a93bd59c42a8641859de1d7620515e71b813
Author: biggiesmallcap-blip <264077051+biggiesmallcap-blip@users.noreply.github.com>
Date:   Tue Jun 2 00:16:39 2026 +1000

    feat(embeddings): add native ollama embedder (#331)

commit 03a268940a331352557009e19ab407a0a115e7ad
Author: biggiesmallcap-blip <264077051+biggiesmallcap-blip@users.noreply.github.com>
Date:   Mon Jun 1 22:58:54 2026 +1000

    fix(dead-code): keep rust sibling test modules live (#332)

commit 43f09e322a54568487c86abd5c7d0658c594d6b6
Author: biggiesmallcap-blip <264077051+biggiesmallcap-blip@users.noreply.github.com>
Date:   Mon Jun 1 22:58:25 2026 +1000

    fix(cli): use shared database engine for reindex (#333)

commit 604d2429026eafaa6be0d65ce396d826d7dadf14
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jun 1 18:21:31 2026 +0530

    docs: sharpen tagline, reframe layer positioning, lift dashboard (#335)
    
    * docs: reframe layer positioning — stop calling our own layers commodity
    
    Drop the 'table stakes' / 'graph and wiki have competition' framing,
    which devalued genuinely strong layers. Elevate the differentiators
    (Decisions, Code Health) with a positive 'Edge' column instead of
    labeling the others as commodities.
    
    * docs: sharpen tagline, reframe layer positioning, lift dashboard
    
    - Tagline now leads with the code-health proof point instead of
      restating the banner.
    - Drop 'table stakes' / 'graph and wiki have competition' framing that
      devalued strong layers; rename the column to 'Edge' with positive
      one-liners and star the two true differentiators.
    - Move the Local dashboard section up to right after Benchmarks so the
      web-UI visual lands higher on the page.

commit 21dffbb47be9f799968f77f055b05db849383a51
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jun 1 17:33:16 2026 +0530

    docs: revamp README and align linked docs for accuracy (#334)
    
    Rewrite the README into a tighter, more skimmable form: a five-layer
    overview table, a dedicated Code Health section leading with the
    head-to-head defect-prediction results, a visual language showcase,
    persona-based routing, and condensed MCP/CLI/dashboard sections. Deep
    prose moves into a new docs/INTELLIGENCE_LAYERS.md.
    
    Correct stale figures across README and linked docs:
    - code-health biomarkers 12/15 -> 25
    - Full-tier languages 8 -> 9, total AST languages 14 -> 15
    - MCP tools 7 -> 9 (document the missing get_symbol and get_health)
    - health-defect benchmark -> 21 repos / 9 languages / mean ROC AUC 0.74
    - add the undocumented risk and delete CLI commands; add init --mode
    
    Add docs/CONFIG.md (the README linked to a file that did not exist) and
    docs/INTELLIGENCE_LAYERS.md.

commit b55e3c5a66cd0b889a1f431661f7ec3efefa2106
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sun May 31 20:09:16 2026 +0530

    fix(cost-tracking): stop second cost engine from wedging `repowise update` on 'database is locked' (#326) (#330)
    
    * fix(cost-tracking): stop second cost engine from wedging update on 'database is locked'
    
    DB-backed cost tracking opens a second SQLAlchemy engine on wiki.db and does an
    INSERT INTO llm_costs per LLM call. During doc generation a writer is held on
    the same file, so each cost insert loses WAL's single-writer race and blocks the
    full 30s busy_timeout before failing — turning a minutes-long `repowise update`
    into an effectively non-terminating one (issue #326).
    
    Two guards:
    
    1. Opt-out: `--no-cost-tracking` flag on `repowise update` and the
       REPOWISE_NO_COST_TRACKING env var (honored by build_cost_tracker, so init and
       the --full upgrade path respect it too). When set, no second engine is opened
       — an in-memory CostTracker still powers the live cost readout.
    
    2. Best-effort persistence when tracking is enabled: the cost engine now uses a
       short (2s) busy_timeout via a new create_engine(busy_timeout_ms=...) override,
       threaded through open_repo_db. A contended cost insert fails fast and is
       dropped by _persist instead of stalling the generation writer. The default
       30s headroom for legitimate bulk graph-edge writes is unchanged.
    
    Tests: per-engine busy_timeout override, env/flag resolution, in-memory
    fallback, and an end-to-end held-write-lock contention test proving record()
    never raises and drops the row under contention while persisting uncontended.
    
    * fix(cost-tracking): defer cost writes out of the generation window by default
    
    Make the default `repowise update` / `init` path safe without requiring the
    opt-out flag. The DB-backed CostTracker used by the CLI is now *buffered*: each
    per-call row is held in memory and persisted in a single transaction by a new
    `flush()` after generation (and KG enrichment) completes — so cost INSERTs never
    land inside the contended generation window at all, for every flow including the
    streaming on_page_ready path. This removes the contention by construction rather
    than merely bounding it.
    
    - CostTracker gains `buffered` (opt-in; default False so external callers such
      as the hosted backend keep immediate writes) and `flush()`; `record()` appends
      to an in-memory buffer when buffered. `_persist_rows` writes the batch in one
      transaction.
    - make_cost_tracker / upgrade_flow build buffered trackers; update_cmd, init_cmd
      (x2) and upgrade_flow flush after generation via a new flush_cost_tracker
      helper. The live cost readout (session_cost) is unaffected.
    
    The short cost-engine busy_timeout and the --no-cost-tracking /
    REPOWISE_NO_COST_TRACKING opt-out from the previous commit remain as defense and
    convenience. Rows still live in wiki.db, so repowise costs and the web/hosted
    readers are unchanged.
    
    Tests: buffered record() writes nothing until flush; flush persists the batch
    after a held writer releases; flush is a no-op for in-memory trackers; the sync
    flush_cost_tracker helper works across asyncio.run loops.

commit 61fce0ab87b3612f465c37ed0b55d5eade12da8f
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sun May 31 16:59:19 2026 +0530

    release: v0.15.2 — on_page_ready streaming callback (#329)
    
    * release: v0.15.2
    
    * docs(changelog): add 0.15.2 entry

commit 5a776a4b27532ae555b35b8ef246d5100c4db04d
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sun May 31 16:52:05 2026 +0530

    feat(generation): add on_page_ready streaming callback (#328)
    
    Thread an optional on_page_ready callback through run_pipeline ->
    run_generation -> generate_all -> the level orchestrator, invoked with
    each GeneratedPage the moment it completes (alongside the existing
    on_page_done, which only receives the page type).
    
    This lets callers persist or stream pages incrementally instead of
    waiting for the whole run to return — e.g. flushing pages to storage
    per page so a generation cut-off yields a partial-but-usable set rather
    than nothing. The callback is best-effort: a sink exception is logged
    and swallowed so it can never drop a page or abort a level.
    
    Additive and fully backward-compatible (defaults to None).

commit 2deede96466abfb5e09a9a2f36e2df5fdce03307
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 18:55:46 2026 +0530

    release: v0.15.1 (#325)
    
    Bump version to 0.15.1 across pyproject, package __init__ files, web
    footer, and uv.lock; add changelog entry.
    
    Patch release shipping two fixes:
    - mcp: surface embedder init failure instead of silent mock fallback (#324)
    - pipeline: drop transient BlameIndex before serialization (#323)

commit 910d374805d2685dcfbb4fa278400a6151f0ead0
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 18:48:31 2026 +0530

    fix(mcp): surface embedder init failure instead of silent mock fallback (#324)
    
    When an embedder is explicitly configured (REPOWISE_EMBEDDER or
    .repowise/config.yaml) but fails to initialise at runtime — most often a
    missing API key — the MCP server swallowed the exception, fell back to
    MockEmbedder with a single WARNING, then reported ready and listed every
    tool. Semantic search (search_codebase, get_answer) silently ran on 8-dim
    mock vectors that cannot match a real index, returning empty/irrelevant
    results with no signal to the caller (#306).
    
    Two fixes:
    
    - Resolve through the shared embedder registry (get_embedder) so every
      backend is honoured — openai, gemini, openrouter, and any custom embedder
      registered via register_embedder. The old code hardcoded only the gemini
      and openai branches, so an explicit `embedder: openrouter` (or any custom
      name) was silently treated as mock with no warning at all.
    
    - Stop pretending to be healthy on failure. An explicitly-configured
      embedder that fails to initialise now logs at ERROR with the missing key
      and remediation, and records the degradation in _state._embedder_status.
      build_meta() surfaces `embedder: "mock"`, `embedder_degraded: true`, and a
      human-readable `embedder_warning` in every tool's _meta envelope so an
      agent can detect the misconfiguration programmatically. MockEmbedder is
      still returned so non-RAG tools keep working; an unconfigured embedder (or
      an explicit `mock`) is the intended default and is never flagged degraded.

commit 9a435b9bdf2ffdac7ceefa9c19e0c4b0e17aeed2
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 18:34:24 2026 +0530

    fix(pipeline): drop transient BlameIndex before serialization (#323)
    
    The per-file BlameIndex is an in-memory-only signal consumed by the health
    biomarkers (function_hotspot / code_age_volatility); its own docstring notes
    it must never round-trip through the DB or artifacts. It was being attached to
    the git-metadata dicts that escape the pipeline in PipelineResult.git_metadata_list,
    so any JSON serialization of git metadata raised "Object of type BlameIndex is
    not JSON serializable", aborting the whole artifact build.
    
    Strip it once the health phase has consumed it, in both the full pipeline
    (orchestrator) and the incremental `update` path, via a shared
    drop_transient_git_signals() helper. DB persistence already filtered it out by
    column, so this is behaviour-preserving for OSS DB users and fixes JSON
    artifact writers (e.g. the hosted backend's git_metadata.json).

commit 300bd5d369992ca9aa3b40c968da56dacfc8826c
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 16:32:10 2026 +0530

    release: v0.15.0 (#321)

commit 23f6cb94095ca00f478558c30bbcddb611d8b3ff
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 16:25:33 2026 +0530

    fix(ui): use extensionless relative imports across package (#320)
    
    The commits/git feature files imported relative modules with explicit
    .js extensions (e.g. "./priority-badge.js"), but the rest of the ui
    package — and the web app's webpack resolution — use extensionless
    relative imports. With no .js->.tsx extension alias configured, those
    specifiers failed to resolve and broke the web build.
    
    Strip the .js extension from all relative import/export specifiers so
    the new files match the package convention and the build resolves.

commit f00e7995d581069d78b45419dbf98868f3f84afe
Author: joptimus <jlewan27@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 03:04:24 2026 -0500

    fix(exclude-patterns): enforce exclude_patterns in git indexer and dynamic edges (#308)
    
    exclude_patterns was honored by file traversal but bypassed by the git indexer
    and by dynamic-edge creation, so excluded files still appeared in git metadata
    and as phantom graph nodes (issues 1 and 2 of #296).
    
    - GitIndexer accepts exclude_patterns and filters them out of `git ls-files`;
      threaded through _run_git_indexing and the orchestrator git stage (init path).
    - The incremental update path passes exclude_patterns to GitIndexer and filters
      changed paths via a new _build_filtered_changed_paths helper.
    - GraphBuilder accepts exclude_patterns; add_dynamic_edges no longer creates
      phantom nodes for excluded targets. Threaded into the ingestion phase and the
      update rebuild path.
    
    Tests: GitIndexer ls-files filtering, _build_filtered_changed_paths, and
    dynamic-edge phantom-node skipping.
    
    Closes #296 (issues 1 and 2)

commit 950269f5dd79d96343633c90927392670881da51
Author: joptimus <jlewan27@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 02:59:06 2026 -0500

    feat(typescript): support .mts/.cts as TypeScript (#310)
    
    Files with the .mts (ESM) and .cts (CommonJS) TypeScript module extensions
    were skipped by the indexer because the TypeScript language spec only mapped
    .ts/.tsx, so they were detected as unknown (issue #288). .cts is included
    because it is the paired CommonJS module extension and shares the parser and
    resolver path.
    
    - Register .mts/.cts in the TypeScript language spec; they propagate to
      EXTENSION_TO_LANGUAGE automatically and route to the plain TypeScript grammar
      (only .tsx needs the JSX variant).
    - Resolve extensionless imports to .mts/.cts across all three resolvers: the
      relative resolver (probe list, directory-barrel /index.mts and /index.cts,
      and the fake-extension fallback), tsconfig path-alias resolution
      (_TS_EXTENSIONS, _INDEX_FILES), and workspace exports (_PROBE_EXTENSIONS).
    - Recognize .mts/.cts test files (.test.mts, .spec.cts, etc.) in every
      test-classification list: dead-code never-flag, health engine _is_test_file
      and _has_paired_test_file, hidden_coupling, coverage detector
      (_TEST_FILE_SUFFIXES and paired_test_file), and coverage_gap.
    
    Out of scope (follow-up): framework-edge route detection for module-script
    extensions (next_app, remix), and full Node ESM/CJS output-mapping semantics.
    
    Closes #288

commit 1ae19dac192fe82aea9afccb80095ccbfaf9c41f
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 13:18:21 2026 +0530

    feat(git): add commits change-risk page, per-function blame, coverage-gradient breakdown (#319)
    
    Surfaces and captures git/health signals the engine produced but never exposed.
    
    Commits / change-risk page (the per-commit table payoff):
    - GET /api/repos/{id}/commits (paginated, sort=risk|date) + /commits/{sha}
      drill-down with an exact per-feature risk-driver breakdown.
    - Normalize change-risk for display: a repo-relative percentile + tercile
      review_priority (RiskNormalizer), so the ranking is portable across repos
      rather than skewing high on repos with large typical commits. The raw
      calibrated score/level are kept but de-emphasized.
    - Persist author_experience on git_commits (migration 0028) so the breakdown
      reproduces the stored score exactly.
    - New packages/ui/src/commits module (review-priority queue, driver breakdown,
      priority badge, credibility strip) + web /commits page + nav entry.
    
    Per-function blame rollup:
    - New git_function_blame table (migration 0029) + rollup builder, persisting the
      per-function modification count, median line age, recent-mod count, and blame
      owner derived from the FULL-tier blame index (previously discarded).
    
    Coverage-gradient surfacing + score breakdown:
    - The score-breakdown endpoint now reads each finding's stored health_impact
      (continuous, weighted, capped) instead of recomputing from the severity band,
      so it reproduces the file score and shows the coverage gradient. coverage_gradient
      records its base deduction; the UI biomarker glossary gains the gradient category.
    
    Incremental / backfill commit capture:
    - Backfill (essential->full) and repowise update now keep git_commits current:
      a bounded walk captures only commits newer than the newest persisted one and
      idempotently upserts; the partial blame rollup is persisted too.

commit dfeeb705539f732449ac0f77381196741c6ecc0c
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 12:05:22 2026 +0530

    feat(git): surface change-complexity and defect-history signals (#318)
    
    Several per-file git signals were computed on every index but never left the
    database: change entropy (Hassan's History Complexity Metric) and its
    percentile, prior bug-fix count, rename lineage, and the temporal hotspot
    score / commit-cap flag (the latter two were already on the hotspots
    endpoint but missing from git-metadata).
    
    Expose them on the git-metadata and hotspots responses (change_entropy_pct
    normalized to 0-100 like churn_percentile), thread them through the shared
    and web TypeScript types, and add a "Change Complexity & Defect History"
    card to the per-file git panel: change entropy as a percentile bar, prior
    bug-fix count, and rename lineage when present. The card renders nothing when
    a file has no such signal.
    
    Pure read-path — no new capture, no re-index. Adds response-shape tests for
    the surfaced fields.

commit 5be3cdadfefe52459bd3582698037074a068a40e
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 11:36:32 2026 +0530

    feat(git): persist per-commit change-risk history (#317)
    
    Add a per-commit `git_commits` table capturing one row per commit in the
    indexed window: author, timestamp, subject, Kamei change features
    (lines/files/dirs/subsystems touched, churn entropy, fix-flag) and a
    calibrated just-in-time change-risk score/level.
    
    The rows are collected during the existing repo-wide commit-index walk via
    an optional sink (no extra git pass) and scored by the existing linear,
    interpretable change-risk model — pure arithmetic, no LLM or blame at
    runtime. Author experience, the one change-risk feature that costs a
    subprocess in the live `repowise risk` path, is reconstructed in-memory as a
    cumulative per-author commit tally, so the whole pass stays zero-extra-git
    (~14 ms for 273 commits on this repo).
    
    Wiring is additive and non-breaking: rows ride on `GitIndexSummary` and are
    upserted alongside file-level git metadata. Includes the model, an Alembic
    migration, bulk-upsert/delete/paginated CRUD (sortable by risk or date), and
    unit + integration coverage.

commit be3ea565161fb94788ebe8b5fe523d2a9f9d94a4
Author: Raghav Chamadiya <raghavchamadiya@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat May 30 10:46:16 2026 +0530

    feat(health): extend code-health biomarkers to Kotlin, C++, and C# (#316)
    
    The code-health complexity walker shipped per-language node maps for
    Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Go, Java, and Rust, but Kotlin, C++, and
    C# had no map — so files in those languages produced no complexity,
    cohesion, or test-quality biomarkers even though they are listed as
    Full-tier. This adds their LanguageNodeMap entries so all nine Full-tier
    languages compute the same biomarker set.
    
    - Kotlin / C++ / C#: control-flow, class-level (LCOM4 / god-class), and
      assertion-block node maps, with handcrafted fixtures and walker tests
      asserting nesting, cyclomatic complexity, class cohesion, and
      assertion-run detection.
    - Walker generalizations (additive; existing languages unchanged):
      follow C/C++ declarator chains to recover the function name; match an
      assertion call that is itself the statement (grammars without an
      expression-statement wrapper); consult the receiver `expression` field
      used by C# member access.
    - Document the implicit-receiver limit (bare `field` vs `this.field`
      degrades cohesion to "no signal", never a false positive).
    - LANGUAGE_SUPPORT.md gains a code-health-biomarker support row plus a
      per-language coverage checklist, and reconciles Go as the one gap
      (no class-grouping node, so no class-level metrics).
    
    No scoring constants change; the snapshot test is untouched.
